range.nativeRange: Southwest Asia (India, Pakistan, Iran); widely cultivated and naturalized globally for thousands of years

usNativeStatus.description: Not native to the US; Mediterranean/Southwest Asian origin; widely cultivated; naturalized in disturbed areas across much of the US but not aggressively

invasive.description: Not invasive; self-seeds readily but is not a regulated species anywhere in the lower 48

zone.description: 2-11 as an annual; self-sowing in most zones

description.height: 3-5' (tall forms); 18-24" (compact dwarf forms); tall, feathery, and airy
description.spread: 12-18" per plant; self-seeds to form a small colony
description.growthRate: Fast annual; mature in 60-90 days from seed
description.timeToMaturity: Foliage harvestable at 40 days; flowers and seeds in 60-90 days
description.lifespan: Annual; self-seeds to maintain colony year after year in a permanent location
description.deciduousEvergreen: Annual — dies with frost; leaves before frost when seeds ripen

lightAndWater.sun: Full sun preferred; tolerates part shade (3-4 hours) though stem elongation increases in shade
lightAndWater.water: Low to medium; drought tolerant once established; excessive watering promotes disease

drainagePreference.description: Well-drained; average garden soil; does not perform in waterlogged conditions

claySoilPerformance.description: Moderate; dill's taproot doesn't develop well in compacted clay; clay drainage improvement with compost helps; raised beds produce better results; tolerates clay better than many herbs if drainage is adequate

soil.texturePreference: Average to well-drained loam; does not require rich soil
soil.rootCharacter: Single taproot — does not transplant well after germination; direct sow in place

bloom.bloomTime: June-August depending on sowing time; succession-sow every 3-4 weeks for continuous harvest
bloom.bloomDescription: Flat compound umbels 4-8" wide; tiny yellow-green flowers; typical Apiaceae structure; the flat open flowers are immediately recognizable; covered with beneficial insects throughout bloom
bloom.fragrance: Strongly aromatic throughout — the classic dill scent from foliage (dill weed) and seeds (dill seed — slightly different, more concentrated aromatic profile)
bloom.pollination: Outstanding insectary plant — the flat open Apiaceae flower structure is accessible to the widest range of beneficial insects; parasitic wasps and hoverflies (key pest predators) work dill flowers intensively; consistently ranks among the top five insectary annuals for the food garden

yield.edibleParts: Foliage ("dill weed" — use fresh in salads, cucumber pickles, potato dishes, sauces, fish; the essential herb for dill pickles); seeds (dried — more concentrated dill flavor; used in pickles, bread, and spice blends); flowers (edible as garnish)
yield.harvestSeason: Foliage from early in the season; harvest before flowering for best leaf flavor; seeds when the seed heads turn brown; dry both immediately
yield.medicinalUses: Traditional digestive herb — relieves gas, bloating, and digestive cramps; mild antispasmodic; "gripe water" for infant colic traditionally contains dill; mild antibacterial properties; used in traditional European and Asian medicine for digestive complaints
yield.craftOtherYield: Dried dill weed; dried dill seed (excellent spice); dill flower heads for pickling

cautionsAndHazards.toxicityPeople: Non-toxic; safe culinary and medicinal herb; CAUTION: contains furanocoumarins (as do most Apiaceae); skin contact with the sap followed by sun exposure may cause phototoxic burns in sensitive individuals — a minor concern in normal culinary use
cautionsAndHazards.toxicityAnimals: Generally safe; small amounts safe for dogs and cats; large quantities may cause mild GI upset
cautionsAndHazards.allelopathy: Dill has documented allelopathic effects on some plants — specifically, do not plant dill adjacent to fennel (they cross-pollinate, producing plants with inferior flavor of both) or carrot family plants (competitive interactions); do not plant near tomatoes once dill begins to flower (the mature plant inhibits tomatoes slightly); young dill plants are beneficial companions for tomatoes

roles: Food (culinary herb — indispensable for pickles and fresh herb use)
Insectary Plant (primary — one of the top annual insectary plants for beneficial pest predators)
Host Plant (for black swallowtail butterfly — a bonus wildlife contribution)
Pest Confuser (aromatic foliage deters some pest insects)
companionPlants: Apple tree guild understory (self-seeding dill colony in the apple guild is a classic insectary planting); cucumbers (the classic dill-cucumber companion for pickling); brassicas (dill attracts wasps that parasitize cabbage worms); tomatoes (beneficial when young; avoid proximity after dill bolts)
antagonists: Fennel (never plant dill near fennel — they cross-pollinate, ruining both; keep 20+ feet apart); mature dill near tomatoes (mild inhibition)
permaculture.coppiceable: Harvest outer stems as needed; the plant naturally terminates in flowering — allow one or two plants to go to seed for the self-seeding colony; the plant self-replaces

wildlife.attracts: Parasitic wasps (Braconidae, Ichneumonidae — key predators of aphids, caterpillars, and other pests), hoverflies (predator larvae eat aphids), lacewings, ground beetles; exceptional beneficial insect attractor
wildlife.hostPlantFor: Black swallowtail butterfly (Papilio polyxenes) — the caterpillars are beautiful green, black, and yellow striped insects that feed on Apiaceae; dill is one of their primary food plants along with parsley, fennel, and carrot; finding swallowtail caterpillars on dill is a pleasure, not a pest problem — leave them and enjoy the butterflies

deer.description: Excellent — strongly aromatic; not a browse target

drought.description: Good once established — tap-rooted; tolerates dry conditions after establishment

tolerances.other: Bolts (goes to seed) quickly in hot weather or drought; sow in succession to maintain foliage harvest; the bolt is actually desirable for insectary and host plant functions

maintenance.pruningNotes: Direct sow where the self-seeding colony is intended; thin to 12-18" spacing after germination. Harvest outer leaves as needed. Allow 1-2 plants to go to seed for the next year's colony. Remove dead plants after seed set. No other management needed — dill self-manages in a stable location.
propagationMethods: Seed (direct sow; does not transplant well after germination; sow succession plantings every 3-4 weeks from April through July for continuous harvest)

cultivarNotes: Several named cultivars exist: **'Bouquet'** (standard tall dill, 3-4', the classic garden form); **'Fernleaf'** (dwarf, 18", slow to bolt — best for limited space or container; AAS award winner); **'Mammoth'** (very tall, 4-5', large seed heads — best for pickling); **'Dukat'** (slow-bolting, high leaf production — best for fresh herb harvest). For the apple guild insectary function, 'Bouquet' or 'Mammoth' (tall forms) are best — more flowers, more insectary value. For kitchen herb use, 'Fernleaf' is convenient for a small space.

suggestedUse: companion plant throughout the vegetable garden
gardenUse.designPlacement: Universal — vegetable garden, herb garden, companion plant, apple guild insectary
gardenUse.acquisitionStatus:
To source (inexpensive annual; direct-sow from seed)
Pennsylvania — Hegins Site Observations:
- None recorded yet

pennsylvaniaHegins.drainage: Hegins: raised beds preferred; in-ground with compost amendment is workable
pennsylvaniaHegins.soil: Raised beds preferred; compost-amended clay is acceptable
pennsylvaniaHegins.cultivarNotes: Best dill cultivars for Zone 6a — 'Bouquet' for general use, 'Fernleaf' for containers; succession sow for continuous harvest
pennsylvaniaHegins.designPlacement: Apple guild companion role; Section B herb strip; raised bed
